lens: Premium pro-tool patterns: full-bleed canvas + on-demand, step-aware detail (how elite diagnostic / CAD-EDA / pro-map / pro-software tools give the workspace full real estate while detail appears on demand and recedes)
keyInsights: RELEVANCE-GRADING beats binary show/hide — and it's the literal mechanic behind 'recede=fade'. ForeFlight's Airspace Highlighting dims airspace your route won't cross and brightens what it will, computed from the flight plan; it never deletes the airspace, so the pilot keeps spatial context. Map this 1:1: the wizard's current step is the 'flight plan'. The lift-pump circuit (lift pump, its connector/pins, 12V feed, ground, fuse/relay, rail-pressure sensor) renders at full opacity/contrast; injectors, FCA, CP4, PCM drop to a single faint background tier (one fade level, not five). The whole truck stays on screen — the tech never loses their place — but only the step's circuit carries visual weight.

STEP REFRAMING is a CAMERA move, not a layout swap — and react-flow already ships the exact API. Mapbox/Google nav frame 'the remaining part of the current LegStep', zoom IN for the maneuver, then zoom OUT for the next leg. The transferable mechanic: on each step, call `fitView({ nodes: [...stepNodeIds], padding: 0.3, duration: 600, interpolate:'smooth' })` (confirmed: fitView accepts a `nodes` filter of {id} objects). The canvas glides to frame ONLY the step's parts; the faded rest scrolls off-frame naturally but is still there if the tech pans out. This is how the diagram 'becomes' the step without a separate stepper UI fighting it — the founder's spine point 2 made literal with one documented call.

CROSS-PROBE HIGHLIGHTING is the established pro-EDA convention for exactly our 'focus one circuit, dim the rest' problem — and it validates the fade direction over deletion. KiCad: when net highlight is active, 'the highlighted net is shown in a brighter color and all other items in a dimmer color.' Altium's Cross-Select Mode dims + masks the non-selected and can auto-zoom to the probed net, configurable as dim-vs-mask. Two steals: (1) treat the active circuit as a 'highlighted net' — brighten its wires/parts, dim everything else as a class; (2) Altium keeps the mask ON during interaction ('Apply Mask During Interactive Editing') so focus doesn't flicker as you work — our fade must PERSIST across taps within a step, only re-resolving when the step advances.

DETAIL IS SHOWN IN THE LOWER REGION ALONGSIDE the connection illustration, NOT in a separate right-rail document — this is the Snap-on ZEUS guided-component-test layout, the closest domain analogue we have. ZEUS sequences: component location -> connector with pin assignments + functions -> normal-operation context -> 'View Meter opens a live meter in the LOWER portion of the screen' beside the connection instructions, with hyperlinks out to specs / known-good waveforms. Steal the spatial model wholesale: the canvas owns the frame; on tap, detail rises from the BOTTOM EDGE (a canvas-anchored sheet) that never covers the part being worked — exactly the founder's 'never sits on top of the part' constraint. Reject our current right-aside <aside> document model in favor of this bottom-anchored, canvas-internal surface.

EXPECTED-vs-ACTUAL as a paired reading is the PicoScope guided-test convention and it's already in our data. Pico's guided tests pre-load 'reference waveforms [that] remain fixed on-screen as a reference against which you compare your captured waveform' so the tech can 'decide whether they are as you would expect.' Our schema carries the same pair: pin.expectedReading (the reference) and scenario.pinReadings[pinId] (the live 'right now'). The step-aware lead (founder point 4) becomes concrete: mid-test on the lift pump's 12V feed, the tap surface LEADS with 'Right now: <live reading>' set directly against 'Expected: ~12V key-on' — reference and actual stacked, Pico-style — with connector/pins/Operational Theori demoted below. Same tap, ordered by the moment.

DIM THE ORIENTATION CHROME, NOT JUST THE DIAGRAM — Linear's 2026 refresh is the principle in writing: 'parts central to the task stay in focus; ones that support orientation and navigation recede... the nav sidebar is a few notches dimmer so the main content takes precedence.' For us the orientation chrome is the header (eyebrow+title+vehicle) and scenario state. Don't delete them (the tech needs to know it's a P0087 on a 6.7 Power Stroke) — render them at a recessed tier (mono, low-contrast, small) so the canvas is unmistakably the bright center of gravity. This directly fixes 'one box among equals': hierarchy by luminance, not by adding borders.

DYNAMIC DECLUTTER BY ZOOM means density should be a function of frame, not fixed. ForeFlight's Continuous Zoom 'materializes' labels/rings/waypoints as you zoom in and sheds them as you zoom out. Apply to pins/labels: when the step frames a tight circuit (zoomed in), show pin role-abbreviations + pin numbers + wire-function labels; when zoomed out to the whole-harness orientation view, suppress pin-level text and show only component names. This keeps the focused frame information-rich and the orientation frame calm — and it's a free outcome of the fitView reframe, since zoom level is known.

FLOATING-ANCHORED detail is contested even at Figma — so anchor to a STABLE canvas edge, not to the moving part. Figma's UI3 moved tools to a floating bottom bar and got sustained backlash ('something put in the way of the design space'; 'submenus hidden right when you need them'). Lesson for our tap-detail: do NOT float a popover pinned to the tapped node's screen position (it jumps as the camera reframes and can occlude the part). Anchor the detail to a fixed canvas edge (bottom on desktop per ZEUS, full bottom-sheet on mobile) so it has a predictable home, never covers the active part, and dismisses on background-tap (our existing onPaneClick already does this).

PROVENANCE belongs INLINE on the reading it qualifies, as a quiet see-source hyperlink — the Snap-on pattern ('hyperlinks that link to other supporting information... specifications, expected results, known-good waveforms'), not a badge wall. Our label map (TRAINING-INFERRED -> 'inferred from theory', GAP -> 'needs field verification' amber, FIELD-VERIFIED -> 'field-verified') should sit as a small mono caption directly under the value it grades, with the whole caption being the see-source affordance (tap -> why-we-believe-this). This keeps 'evidence not oracle' true without the emoji/pill aesthetic the brief rejects.

concreteDirection: Make the canvas a STEP CAMERA over a relevance-graded graph, with a bottom-anchored pull surface — no document chrome competing for space. Concretely: (1) The screen is the diagram, full-bleed; the header/vehicle/scenario become a thin recessed mono ribbon (Linear-dim), not stacked blocks. Delete the boxed canvas-wrap and the right <aside>. (2) Per wizard step, compute a stepNodeIds set (lift pump + its connector/pins + 12V feed + ground + fuse/relay + rail-pressure sensor for the P0087 lift-pump-feed step) and call `fitView({ nodes: stepNodeIds.map(id => ({id})), padding: 0.3, duration: 600, interpolate:'smooth' })` (Mapbox LegStep reframe, react-flow native). (3) Everything outside stepNodeIds renders at one faint background tier — opacity ~0.28, desaturated wire color, pin labels suppressed (ForeFlight dim + KiCad dim-the-rest); fade PERSISTS across taps, re-resolving only when the step advances (Altium persistent mask). (4) Tap a part -> a bottom-anchored canvas-internal sheet rises (ZEUS lower-screen meter; NOT a floating popover, NOT a side document) that never covers the framed part. Its content order is STEP-AWARE: mid-test on the 12V feed it LEADS with 'Right now: <live reading>' set against 'Expected: ~12V key-on' (PicoScope expected-vs-actual), then connector/# pins/what-each-wire-does/location, then the short Operational Theori. Every value carries a quiet mono see-source caption ('inferred from theory' / amber 'needs field verification' / 'field-verified') that is itself the why-we-believe-this link. (5) Visual language from the existing kit: bone bg, serif for the part name + Operational Theori prose, mono for readings/labels/provenance, the faint OKLCH graph grid already in v2.css (NOT react-flow's default dotted Background — replace it). Curator-only: the captured/missing summary appears as a collapsed mono affordance in the recessed ribbon, role-gated, invisible to techs/owners.
critiqueOfCurrent: The current screen is a stacked DOCUMENT with a widget in it, and every elite tool surveyed inverts that. (1) The diagram is boxed (.topo__canvas-wrap) and sits as one block beneath header + ScenarioBar + 'Now showing' strip and beside a right <aside> — five elements competing for room, the exact opposite of ZEUS/ForeFlight where the canvas owns the frame and detail lives within it. (2) ZERO step-awareness: selection is a flat 4-way explorer (empty|component|pin|connection) with no concept of an active step, so nothing fades, nothing reframes — it cannot express the founder's governing rule at all. The whole fuel harness renders at equal weight while the tech traces one circuit (the precise pain the brief names). (3) The right <aside> is a persistent document panel — it pushes a full architecture-facts dump (Location/Function/Electrical/Probe points/all test branches/full pin list) regardless of where the tech is, violating both 'pull not push' and 'every word counts'; ZEUS/Pico show only the active test's reading first. (4) react-flow's default dotted Background + default Controls + default node boxes + light colorMode are the rejected default-AI aesthetic verbatim; the repo already has a better grid + token system in v2.css that the canvas bypasses. (5) Provenance renders as a standalone topo-panel__provenance chip rather than inline on the reading it qualifies, drifting toward the badge-wall the brief forbids.
pitfalls: Reframing on EVERY tap (camera chases each selection) — strobes the view and disorients the tech. The camera moves on STEP CHANGE only; within a step, taps just raise the bottom sheet. (Altium keeps the mask stable during interactive work for this reason.)

Fading too hard (the rest of the harness effectively vanishes) — breaks the founder's fade-not-vanish rule and the tech loses spatial place. One faint-but-readable tier (~0.28 opacity, still has shape), never display:none, never a separate 'collapsed' view.

Floating the tap-detail as a popover pinned to the node — it jumps when the camera reframes and can occlude the very part being probed. Anchor to a fixed canvas edge (Figma UI3 backlash + the founder's 'never on top of the part').

Pushing the full architecture-facts dump on tap (porting the current <aside> wholesale) — re-violates 'pull not push / every word counts'. The sheet shows the step-led fact first; deeper facts are present but secondary and below the fold.

Letting fitView's nodes filter frame too tightly with no padding — the tech loses the immediate neighbors that give the circuit meaning. padding ~0.3 keeps adjacent faded context in-frame (ForeFlight keeps near-route airspace visible).

Mistaking 'step-aware' for a visible 'step N of M' stepper — banned by standing principle. The canvas IS the step's expression; there is no progress UI, only what's done (already-resolved circuits can stay lightly visible) + what's now (the framed circuit).
Surfacing the curator captured/missing summary to techs/owners — role leak. It is curator-access only; gate it server-side, not just hidden with CSS.
Replacing the dotted grid with ANOTHER decorative grid — the v2.css OKLCH graph-paper grid is the quiet, grounded choice; don't add a second visual texture.
mobileNotes: At 375-414px the camera model degrades cleanly because it's already 'frame one thing'. (1) The recessed header ribbon collapses to a single line: vehicle + code, mono, ~11px (the eyebrow drops). (2) fitView's per-step framing is MORE valuable on mobile — frame the step's 5-6 nodes so they're legibly large on a phone; the faded rest is mostly off-frame but a pinch-out still reveals it (don't lock zoom). Use a tighter padding (~0.15) and a higher minZoom floor than desktop so the focused circuit fills the narrow viewport. (3) The bottom-anchored detail sheet becomes a true bottom sheet (our existing mobile bottom-sheet pattern) — it already lives at the bottom edge, so the ZEUS lower-screen model and the mobile pattern converge; it covers the lower ~55% max, leaving the framed part visible above it, and dismisses on backdrop-tap. (4) Scenario controls collapse from a horizontal bar to a single 'Now showing' chip that opens a sheet on tap — no horizontal scroll-bar of scenarios. (5) Pin labels: at phone zoom show role-abbreviation only (e.g. 'B+'), full wire-function text moves into the tap sheet (ForeFlight materialize-on-zoom). (6) Provenance see-source caption stays inline under the reading and remains tappable at >=44px target height. The hard gate: a tech on a phone in the bay must be able to read the live-vs-expected reading for the lift-pump 12V feed without horizontal scrolling and without the sheet covering the pin they're probing.

lens: Cognitive-load-per-step: the minimum a tech must see at each step type, and what leads / recedes on the lift-pump 12V-feed test
keyInsights: EXPERTISE REVERSAL is the load-bearing research here: for a NOVICE, extra context (full harness, every pin, theory prose) aids comprehension; for an EXPERT under time pressure it INVERTS into harm. Sweller/Kalyuga's expertise-reversal effect (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21443379) shows redundant guidance forces a high-knowledge person to spend working memory attending-to and integrating information they already hold, starving the actual task. Vyntechs users are master diesel techs. So the canvas's default is NOT 'show, then let them filter' — it is 'show the single thing this step needs, and treat every extra element as a measured cost.' This is the scientific spine under Brandon's 'unneeded info actively causes pain.'

Define the MINIMUM per step TYPE, because each type has a different single job. ORIENT (start of a sub-circuit): one job = 'which neighborhood am I in.' Minimum = the focused circuit cluster named, rest faded, NO readings, NO pins exposed. LOCATE-A-PART: one job = 'find this physical thing on the truck.' Minimum = the one part spotlit + its location string (e.g. 'frame rail, driver side, ahead of fuel tank'); pins/readings stay collapsed. PROBE-A-PIN: one job = 'put the meter HERE.' Minimum = the one pin lit on its connector + probeLocation + the meter mode; expectedReading present but visually quieter. INTERPRET-A-READING: one job = 'is my number right.' Minimum = MY-number-vs-expected side by side + a single in/out-of-range verdict; missingLogic deferred until out-of-range. BRANCH: one job = 'where next.' Minimum = the verdict + the ONE next part lighting up; alternatives the system didn't pick are never shown (honors the no-'step N of M' rule).

For the REPRESENTATIVE lift-pump 12V-feed test ('back-probe the lift pump power feed, key-on'), the step type is PROBE-then-INTERPRET. The ONE thing that LEADS on tap of the lift-pump connector is the live expected reading for THIS feed in THIS scenario: 'Key-on: ~12.4 V at the B+ feed pin' — the number and where to put the probe (pin.expectedReading + pin.probeLocation, scenario.pinReadings). SECONDARY-ON-DEMAND (present, dimmer, one tap away): connector type + pin count, what each wire does, location, the Operational Theori snippet (component.role/wireSummary/body). WHAT FADES: injectors, FCA, CP4 high-pressure pump, PCM — all to faint background; even the rail-pressure sensor stays only as a faint labeled 'why we're here' anchor, NOT a live readout competing with the feed pin. This is the step-aware tap-ordering Brandon resolved, made literal.

The 'recede = fade, not vanish' rule is validated by COGNITIVE-TUNNELING + DECLUTTER-MODE research from aviation HUDs. Wickens/Thomas show that compelling foreground symbology causes pilots to tunnel and lose the outside scene; the fix shipped in real cockpits is a DECLUTTER MODE that reduces — not removes — competing instruments. Fade-don't-vanish is exactly declutter-mode for a wiring harness: the tech keeps peripheral place in the whole truck (no disorientation, no 'where did the rest go') while the active circuit owns foreground attention. Pure hide would risk the opposite failure — losing spatial context. So the spine is HCI-correct, and the craft job is tuning the fade depth (faded parts must be unmistakably 'not now' yet still locate-able).

Change-blindness research (Heer & Robertson on animated transitions; NN/g change-blindness) dictates HOW the canvas moves between steps, and it is a HARD requirement not polish: when the focus circuit changes, an INSTANT swap makes the expert miss what moved (they were heads-down on a meter). A staged, ~300-400ms animated transition that preserves object constancy — the lift-pump cluster brightening + sliding toward focus while the harness dims in place — lets the tech track the change without re-scanning the whole diagram. Heer's finding: staged transitions reduce object-tracking error and improve understanding vs. instant changes. The recede/focus motion is the single most important load-reducer between steps.

REDUCE DECISIONS-PER-MOMENT by pre-computing the one next move, the way Google Maps turn-by-turn does. Maps doesn't show the whole route at decision scale — it smart-zooms to the imminent maneuver, shows ONE instruction, and only widens for genuinely tricky turns (9to5google.com/2026/03/12 immersive navigation). The diagnostic equivalent: at the lift-pump step the canvas is zoomed/framed to that circuit, states the one action, and the tech makes zero navigation decisions. They never choose 'what to look at next' — the walk's current step already chose. This directly satisfies the standing 'system computes next silently' principle: the silence IS the load reduction.

INTERPRET-A-READING should be a known-good COMPARISON, not a raw number, modeled on PicoScope guided tests (picoauto.com/library/automotive-guided-tests). Pico auto-applies settings and shows ONE reference waveform fixed on screen so the tech compares captured-vs-known-good side by side — the interpretation is offloaded from memory to perception. For the 12V feed: show the tech's expected '~12.4 V' as the reference and let their observed value sit against it, with a single binary verdict (in-range / low). The expensive cognitive step — 'what does a wrong number mean here' (pin.missingLogic, e.g. 'low/no voltage = blown fuel pump relay or open in feed; check the relay before condemning the pump') — stays DEFERRED until the reading is actually out of range. Don't pre-load the failure tree onto a passing test.

PROVENANCE must be a PERIPHERAL, low-salience cue, never a competing foreground element — otherwise it becomes its own cognitive-tunneling source on every part. Per the HUD 8-degree-separation finding, source grade ('inferred from theory' / 'needs field check' amber) should ride as a quiet inline mark on the detail surface, with a one-tap 'why we believe this / see source' path — present for trust, silent until queried. The amber GAP cue is the ONE exception that earns elevated salience, because an unverified value the tech is about to act on is genuinely task-relevant (it changes whether they trust the number) — amber is signal, not decoration, at exactly the moment of a probe.

SIGNAL-VS-NOISE for an expert means cutting the labels an expert already knows. Per expertise-reversal, redundant naming (re-labeling 'connector,' 'ground,' 'B+' on parts a master tech reads instantly) is noise that costs working memory. The detail surface's hard rule — 'short and concise, every word counts' (Brandon's content rule) — is the correct cognitive-load discipline: lead with the number and the action verb, drop the explanatory scaffolding novices would need. The Operational Theori snippet earns its place ONLY because it's pulled on tap (declared intent), never pushed.

concreteDirection: Build the canvas as a STEP-DRIVEN declutter engine with a strict per-step-type information budget. The walk's current step emits a focus set (the active circuit's components + the one active pin) and a step TYPE (orient / locate / probe / interpret / branch). The canvas does three things, in this order: (1) FADE everything outside the focus set to a faint, still-legible background (declutter mode, not hide); (2) staged ~350ms animated transition that brightens-and-pulls the focus circuit toward center while the harness dims in place, preserving object constancy so the heads-down tech can track what moved; (3) expose ONLY the information this step type needs. For the representative lift-pump 12V-feed PROBE/INTERPRET step: the lift-pump cluster (lift pump, its connector, the 12V B+ feed pin, ground, the feeding fuse/relay) is focused; the rail-pressure sensor stays as a faint labeled 'why we're here' anchor; injectors/FCA/CP4/PCM fade out. Tapping the lift-pump connector PULLS an anchored detail surface (not a fixed aside) whose LEADING line is the step-relevant reading: 'Key-on — expect ~12.4 V at B+ feed' + where to put the probe; observed-vs-expected shown as a known-good comparison (PicoScope pattern) with a single in/low verdict. Connector type, pin count, per-wire function, location, and the Operational Theori snippet are present but visually secondary, one level down. The failure tree (missingLogic) stays collapsed until the reading is out of range. Provenance rides as a quiet inline mark with a one-tap see-source path; amber 'needs field check' is the only cue allowed to raise salience, and only because an unverified value the tech is about to act on is genuinely task-relevant. No 'step N of M', no alternatives the system didn't pick — the next part simply lights up when this test resolves.
critiqueOfCurrent: The current stacked page is the textbook expertise-reversal violation: it shows a master diesel tech the WHOLE harness, every node, every pin, a static scenario bar, a persistent 'now showing' strip, AND a captured/missing footer all at once, with zero step-awareness (selection is a flat empty|component|pin|connection explorer). Every element that isn't the active test is, per Sweller/Kalyuga, redundant information the expert must actively attend-to and suppress — burning the working memory the actual diagnosis needs. The right-hand <aside> detail panel is PUSHED standing chrome that competes for room beside the map and dumps all of a component's facts in fixed order (location, function, electrical, probe points, every test, every branch) regardless of where the tech is in the walk — the opposite of step-aware tap-ordering. The 'now showing' strip and captured/missing footer are foreground competitors that, per cognitive-tunneling research, fragment attention. And because the diagram is one box among equals rather than the workspace, the tech is forced to make navigation decisions ('what do I look at') the tool should be making silently. Nothing fades, nothing focuses, nothing reorders by moment — it is a reference document, not a step-aware tool.
pitfalls: Building a generic 'highlight selected, dim rest' interaction instead of a per-step-TYPE information budget. Locate, probe, and interpret need DIFFERENT minimums; one uniform highlight rule re-introduces redundant info on the steps where it's noise (e.g. showing the live reading during a pure LOCATE step the tech only needs the location for).

Fading too hard into vanish. If faded parts become unreadable or unfindable, you trade cognitive-tunneling for disorientation — the tech loses spatial place in the truck (the exact failure aviation declutter-mode avoids by reducing, not removing). Tune fade so 'not now' is unmistakable yet the part is still locate-able and tappable.

Instant (zero-duration) focus swaps between steps. A heads-down tech reading a meter will miss what moved and have to re-scan the whole diagram (change blindness). Without a staged ~300-400ms object-constancy transition, the focus mechanic actively raises load instead of lowering it.

Dumping the failure tree (missingLogic / branches) onto a passing or not-yet-taken test. Pre-loading 'what a wrong reading means' on an INTERPRET step that's still in-range is redundant guidance for an expert — defer it until the reading is actually out of range.

Turning provenance into a badge wall. A loud source-grade chip on every part becomes its own foreground attention sink (cognitive tunneling) and inverts trust into clutter. Keep it peripheral and on-demand; reserve raised salience for amber GAP at the moment of a probe only.

Letting the part-detail surface become persistent or land on top of the active part. The moment it stops being pull-on-tap and becomes a standing panel, it crosses Brandon's theory-of-operation 'never a side panel' line AND re-creates the competing-chrome problem the current aside has.

Over-trimming for the expert and orphaning the LOCATE step. Master techs know the system but may not know THIS truck's exact part location — the location string is high-value signal, not redundant noise. Don't cut it as 'something they already know.'
mobileNotes: At 375-414px there is no room for fade-in-place plus an anchored detail surface side by side, so the information budget must get STRICTER, not just reflow. Keep the staged focus transition (it costs no width and is the primary load-reducer) but make the focus reframe a true smart-zoom — the lift-pump cluster fills the viewport, the rest of the harness collapses to a small persistent 'place keeper' (a faded mini-strip or edge indicator the tech can tap to zoom back out), preserving spatial context the way Maps keeps a route-overview affordance. The tap-detail surface becomes a bottom sheet that opens to a FIRST height showing only the leading step-relevant line (the reading + probe location + verdict) — the secondary tier (connector, pins, wire functions, Operational Theori) lives below the fold and is revealed by dragging the sheet up (progressive disclosure, NN/g's mobile-usability win). The bottom sheet must never cover the active part: on phone, focus-zoom the active pin into the top third of the viewport before opening the sheet from the bottom, so the part and its leading number are both visible. The known-good comparison stacks vertically (expected over observed) instead of side-by-side. Provenance stays a single inline mark, amber GAP still allowed to raise salience. Per-step minimum is the mobile salvation: because each step shows only one circuit and one leading fact, the small screen is sufficient — it's the current all-at-once page that can't survive 375px, not the step-aware one.

lens: Step-aware canvas mechanics (focus / fade / reframe as the walk advances)
keyInsights: THE PRIMITIVE ALREADY EXISTS, UNUSED. The canvas runs @xyflow/react and `fitView` accepts `{ nodes: [...], duration, padding, maxZoom, interpolate: 'smooth', ease }` (reactflow.dev/api-reference/types/fit-view-options). So 'reframe to the active circuit' is literally `reactFlowInstance.fitView({ nodes: stepNodeIds, duration: 520, padding: 0.28, maxZoom: 1.15, interpolate: 'smooth' })`. No new layout engine, no second canvas. The dagre layout (topology-layout.ts, rankdir TB, PCM as hub) stays fixed; only the VIEWPORT moves. This is the spine's 'diagram IS the workspace' made literal — one persistent coordinate space, the camera narrates it.

TWO-LAYER FOCUS, NOT ONE. Real focus UIs separate the CAMERA move from the EMPHASIS change, and run them together. Layer 1 = reframe (fitView to step nodes). Layer 2 = spatial dim (non-step nodes drop to ~12-15% opacity, step nodes stay 100%). Doing only one is the failure: dim-without-reframe leaves the tech hunting a small bright cluster in a big frame; reframe-without-dim crops neighbors at the edge that still shout for attention. Run both on the SAME ~500ms ease and the eye reads it as a single 'the truck leaned toward the lift pump' motion. The codebase ALREADY ships the edge half of this: topology.css has `.wire.dim { opacity: 0.25 }` and `.wire.is-active` (lines 304-308); only NODE-level dim and the camera move are net-new.

FADE ≠ VANISH IS LITERALLY THE OPACITY FLOOR. The spine says receded parts fade to a faint background and never disappear — so the dim target must be a NON-ZERO floor (~0.12-0.15 opacity, never 0, never display:none). This is also what keeps mobile honest: a faded CP4/PCM at the frame edge is the 'you are here in the whole truck' anchor. Tie the floor to a token (e.g. `--topo-recede: 0.13`) so it's one knob. Existing wire `.off` state already sits at 0.16 (topology.css:290) — the node floor should sit just under it so faded STRUCTURE reads quieter than a live-but-off wire.

ANCHOR = GOOGLE MAPS' followMyLocation ↔ showRouteOverview TOGGLE. Maps Navigation SDK exposes exactly two camera modes — tight follow vs. whole-route overview (developers.google.com/maps/documentation/navigation/android-sdk/camera). That is the orientation answer: default to FOLLOW (framed on the lift-pump circuit), and give one always-present affordance — a 'show whole system' button (the Maps overview-chip equivalent) that `fitView()`s to ALL nodes and lifts the dim, then snaps back to the step on next interaction. The tech never gets lost because the whole-truck view is one tap away, but it's never the default mid-test.

'SMART ZOOM / LOOK AHEAD' = maxZoom CLAMP + asymmetric padding. Maps Immersive Navigation reframes per-maneuver and deliberately zooms OUT for tricky turns so you see context (9to5google.com/2026/03/12, blog.google immersive-navigation). Translate: clamp `maxZoom` (~1.1-1.2) so a single-node step (just the lift pump) doesn't balloon to fill the screen and lose its wires; and use ASYMMETRIC padding to leave room where the pulled detail will land (more bottom-padding on mobile where the sheet rises, more right-padding on desktop where detail anchors). fitView's padding accepts per-side values, so the framed circuit sits OFF-CENTER, clearing the space the detail occupies — satisfying spine rule 5 ('never sits on top of the part the tech is working on').

REFRAME IS TRIGGERED BY STEP CHANGE, NOT BY TAP. Critical distinction the current flat-explorer model can't express: a TAP (pull detail) must NOT move the camera — that would yank the truck out from under the tech mid-probe. The camera reframes only when the WALK ADVANCES (step → step) or when the tech explicitly hits 'show whole system.' Within a step, tapping parts only swaps the pulled detail + re-points the active/dim emphasis among already-framed nodes. This mirrors Figma: selecting a layer doesn't move your viewport; only the explicit Shift+2 'zoom to selection' does (help.figma.com keyboard). Selection ≠ navigation.

TAPPING A FADED PART IS A FIRST-CLASS, DEFINED GESTURE. Per spine, faded parts are present and live. Define it: tapping a faded part PULLS its detail (one-part, every-word-counts) but does NOT reframe and does NOT change the active step — it briefly raises that one part from the recede-floor to ~0.55 ('peek') so the tech sees what they touched, then it settles back when detail closes. This lets a tech sanity-check 'is the CP4 downstream of this?' without leaving the lift-pump step. It's the canvas equivalent of Maps letting you pan away to look ahead, then recentering.

GOING BACK = REVERSE THE SAME CAMERA MOVE; STATE IS ADDITIVE. Per the standing 'show only done + now, system computes next silently' rule, back-a-step must NOT feel like undo-with-loss. Re-running `fitView({nodes: prevStepNodes})` with the same easing reverses the camera smoothly (reveal.js auto-animate / PowerPoint Morph prove a viewport tween reads as reversible movement, not a cut — revealjs.com/auto-animate). Any field-verified readings the tech already captured stay rendered on their wires when the camera returns; the back-step shows what's DONE there, never a fresh empty step. No 'step 2 of 6' — the only signals are the framed circuit + captured readings.

REDUCED-MOTION IS A HARD BRANCH, NOT A NICETY. Camera pans are textbook vestibular triggers (web.dev/articles/prefers-reduced-motion; esri arcgis-blog). Under `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`, set fitView `duration: 0` (instant cut to the framed circuit) and keep ONLY the opacity cross-fade for emphasis — opacity fades are explicitly the safe substitute. The destination framing is identical; only the tween is dropped. This is a real degradation, not a kill-switch.

concreteDirection: Add a STEP MODEL on top of the existing flat selection. Each step declares `nodeIds` (lift pump, its connector, the 12V-feed wire, ground, the feeding fuse/relay, plus the rail-pressure sensor as the downstream symptom source) and ONE lead fact (the reading for the back-probe). The canvas keeps ONE persistent dagre coordinate space; the WALK drives the CAMERA, not the layout. On step entry, run a single ~500ms eased move that does two things together: (1) `reactFlowInstance.fitView({ nodes: step.nodeIds, duration: 520, padding: {top:0.18, right:0.32, bottom:0.34, left:0.2}, maxZoom: 1.15, interpolate: 'smooth', ease: easeOutCubic })` — asymmetric padding reserves the corner where pulled detail lands so it never covers the worked part; (2) cross-fade every non-step node to a `--topo-recede: 0.13` floor while step nodes hold 100% (reuse the existing `.wire.dim` / `.wire.is-active` mechanism, extend it to nodes). NEVER display:none — faded parts are the whole-truck anchor. A TAP pulls one-part detail and never moves the camera; tapping a FADED part 'peeks' it (recede-floor → ~0.55) without changing the step. Provide ONE persistent 'show whole system' affordance (Maps overview-chip) that fitView()s to all nodes + lifts dim, auto-returning to the step on the next advance/tap. BACK = re-run fitView on the previous step's nodes (camera reverses smoothly); captured field-verified readings stay rendered on their wires. Under `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`, set all fitView durations to 0 (instant frame) and keep only the opacity cross-fade. No 'step N of M'; the only progress signals are the framed circuit + the captured readings left behind.
critiqueOfCurrent: The current canvas has ZERO step-awareness — selection is a flat explorer (empty|component|pin|connection in topology-diagnostic.tsx) and the camera only ever calls `fitView` once on mount to fit ALL nodes (topology-diagram.tsx: bare `fitView` + FIT_VIEW_OPTIONS minZoom 0.7). So the tech back-probing the lift pump's 12V feed is staring at the ENTIRE fuel harness (injectors, FCA, CP4, PCM) at equal weight — a direct violation of the governing 'only what's needed now' rule, and exactly the mid-job pain the spine names. Dim exists for WIRES only (`.wire.dim`), driven by pin selection, not by a step — and nothing dims the NODES, so even a selected pin leaves every component box at full strength. There's no orientation anchor, no whole-system toggle, no reframe, no concept of advancing or going back. It reads as a static diagram with a click-to-inspect widget bolted on, not a tool that re-expresses itself around the work. The camera is the single biggest unused lever: the library can already narrate the walk and the code never asks it to.
pitfalls: Over-zooming a single-node step: framing just 'lift pump' with no maxZoom clamp balloons one box to fill the screen and severs its wires from context — defeats the spatial point. Always clamp maxZoom (~1.1-1.2) and include the connecting wires/neighbors of interest in step.nodeIds.

Reframing on TAP: moving the camera when the tech taps a part yanks the truck out from under them mid-probe. Tap pulls detail only; camera moves only on step-advance or explicit overview. Keep selection decoupled from navigation (the Figma rule).

display:none / opacity:0 on receded parts: violates 'recede = fade not vanish,' destroys the whole-truck anchor, and breaks edges that route through hidden nodes. Use a non-zero recede floor (~0.13), never removal.

Animating per-node with staggered CSS instead of one viewport tween: produces a chaotic 'everything reshuffles' effect. The layout must stay FIXED (dagre positions never change); only the camera + opacity move, on one shared ease.
Forgetting reduced-motion: a 500ms pan/zoom is a literal vestibular trigger. Missing the duration:0 branch is an accessibility regression, not a polish gap.
Letting the back-step render as an empty/fresh step: violates 'show only done + now.' Returning to a prior step must show its captured readings, not re-prompt work already finished.

Auto-returning the overview to the wrong step: if 'show whole system' is open and the walk advances in the background, snapping to a stale step is disorienting — always return to the CURRENT step, not the one that was active when overview opened.
mobileNotes: At 375-414px the reframe matters MORE, not less — there's no room to show the whole harness, so the camera doing the work is the only way the canvas is usable. Use heavier BOTTOM padding in fitView (e.g. bottom: 0.42) so the framed lift-pump circuit sits in the UPPER portion and the detail bottom-sheet (existing mobile pattern) rises into reserved empty space, never over the worked part (spine rule 5). The 'show whole system' affordance becomes a thumb-reachable chip at a bottom corner, not a desktop hover control. maxZoom can run slightly higher on mobile (~1.3) because a tight circuit of 3-5 parts is exactly what fits a phone — but never so tight that pins lose their labels. Keep the recede-floor at 0.13 so the faded harness is still the 'whole truck' orientation cue on a small screen where panning to find your place is painful. Reduced-motion is disproportionately common on phones (battery/accessibility), so the duration:0 instant-frame branch must be tested at 375px specifically. The 'peek a faded part' gesture is a tap (already touch-native); ensure peeked detail in the bottom sheet is dismissible with a downward swipe so the tech returns to the step in one gesture.

lens: Theming & visual language — a premium "blueprint instrument" system harvested from the in-repo vt kit, extended for the step-aware canvas
keyInsights: REUSE — the token foundation already exists and is excellent; do NOT invent a parallel palette. app/globals.css defines the whole 'Workshop Instrument' system the canvas should feel native to: bone surface ramp (--vt-bone-50 oklch(98.5% 0.008 80) page through --vt-bone-900 oklch(18% 0.010 55) ink), a single navy accent (--vt-signal-500 oklch(50% 0.18 248) — 'blueprint ink on bond paper'), the ignition amber (--vt-amber-500 oklch(80% 0.215 78)), a calibrated risk ramp (--vt-risk-high oklch(72% 0.19 55) ... --vt-risk-destructive oklch(62% 0.22 25)), a domain element palette (--vt-elem-fuel green 140, --vt-elem-voltage chartreuse 108, --vt-elem-coolant 220, --vt-elem-vacuum violet 300), three faces (--vt-font-serif Instrument Serif = body/voice, --vt-font-mono JetBrains Mono = ALL data/readings/pins, --vt-font-sans Inter Tight = micro-chrome only), small radii (--vt-radius-1 2px .. --vt-radius-3 6px; 'workshop instruments don't have pillows'), and calm motion tokens (--vt-ease-instrument cubic-bezier(.4,0,.2,1), --vt-dur-2 160ms / --vt-dur-3 280ms). The canvas's job is to SPEND these tokens better, not mint new ones.

BUG/ADD — the wire palette is currently undefined and renders colorless. components/topology/topology.css references --role-signal, --role-12v, --role-ground, --role-pwm, --role-5v-ref, --role-low-ref on every .wire--* rule AND on the fault readout (.topo__readout.is-fault b, .topo-panel__alarm) — but these six tokens are defined NOWHERE (not in globals.css :root, not v2.css, not set inline on the canvas in topology-diagram.tsx). So today wires fall back to the SVG default and the whole 'color = electrical role' language is dead on arrival. The single highest-leverage theming ADD is to define these six as first-class semantic tokens mapped onto the existing element ramp: --role-12v -> a warm red/orange near --vt-risk-high (power = hot), --role-ground -> --vt-fg-2/graphite (return = neutral ink), --role-signal -> --vt-elem-voltage chartreuse, --role-5v-ref -> --vt-elem-coolant cyan, --role-low-ref -> a desaturated cyan, --role-pwm -> --vt-elem-vacuum violet. Wire color must be a TOKEN, dark-mode-proof and reusable app-wide.

REPLACE the node box, don't restyle it. The current .topo-node is the rejected default: a 210x86 hard-bordered (1.4px solid --vt-fg) bone rectangle with name/kind/location stacked — generic card-soup that screams react-flow default. Premium pro-tool/CAD convention is a faceplate, not a card: kill the heavy full border, use a thin 0.5px hairline (--vt-rule-strong) like the rest of the vt kit (every vt divider is 0.5px), set the component NAME in Instrument Serif at 15-16px, the kind/location in mono micro-caps (the existing .topo-node__kind 9px 0.1em uppercase mono is right), and let the pins be the only high-contrast geometry on the plate. Selection should not be a 2px amber border (current .is-selected) — it should be a navy ink halo + faint signal-tint fill, matching how --vt-accent-bg oklch(50% 0.18 248 / 0.10) is used elsewhere. The plate reads like an engraved instrument label, echoing the existing .vt-plate engraved-plate component in v2-instruments.css.

MOTION/FOCUS — the recede-and-spotlight craft should reuse the kit's calm-motion contract, not bouncy AI transitions. The system already declares 'MOTION — calm. No bounces. Quick, decisive.' with --vt-ease-instrument and a 4-step duration ramp. Step transitions (the canvas reframing around the lift-pump circuit) should be a single coordinated 280-480ms (--vt-dur-3/4) ease on TWO properties only: opacity (faded parts -> ~0.18-0.25, matching the existing .wire.off opacity:0.16 and .wire.dim opacity:0.25 which ALREADY encode the recede language) and a react-flow viewport fitView tween to the active circuit's bounding box. No scale-pop on nodes, no color flashes. The faded background is a real, already-shipped value (0.18-0.25) — adopt it as the canonical 'receded' opacity token (--vt-recede: 0.2) so fade is consistent for nodes, wires, and labels.

PROVENANCE color must be SEMANTIC and quiet, and amber is the domain-correct choice. The brief's amber='needs field check' is not arbitrary: ISO 2575 / SAE J578 codify amber/yellow = caution/advisory ('check soon, not yet critical') vs red = stop-now critical across every dashboard a diesel tech has ever read. So the gap signal should be --vt-amber-500 (oklch(80% 0.215 78)) used as a thin hairline + micro-cap label ('needs field check'), NEVER a filled pill (rejected). 'inferred from theory' = neutral graphite ink (--vt-fg-3), no color at all — it's the default state, not an alarm. 'field-verified' = a single quiet --vt-signal-500 tick. Reserve --vt-risk-high/destructive (red) for actual fault readings (the P0087 low-pressure state), so the tech's eye learns: red=measured-wrong, amber=we-don't-know-yet, ink=drafted. The existing .topo-panel__provenance[data-provenance='GAP'] already wires amber — keep that, extend it as the app-wide provenance ramp.

TYPOGRAPHY — enforce the three-face discipline the kit already mandates, which is most of the premium feel for free. The rule (from globals.css): mono for ALL numeric/measured data (pin readings, expected ranges, pin numbers, the live 'now showing' value — with font-feature-settings:'tnum' tabular so digits don't jitter as readings update, exactly the instrument-readout convention), Instrument Serif for the part name and the one-line Operational Theori (editorial voice, the 'every word counts' prose), Inter Tight ONLY for micro ALL-CAPS eyebrows/labels (.vt-eyebrow 11px 0.12em). The current detail panel mostly honors this; the canvas labels do NOT yet use tnum on readings. Tabular numerals on the live readout is the difference between 'gauge' and 'web text'.

The active-wire focus mechanic is a SOLVED pattern — steal Mapbox's 3-layer route line + Altium/KiCad net-highlight. Mapbox renders a route as casing (wide dark underlay) -> main line -> traffic, and dims inactive legs via inActiveRouteLegsColor. KiCad net-highlight brightens the selected net and dims everything else; Altium draws a highlight OVER the active wire and makes the rest semi-transparent. For the lift-pump 12V feed: render the active circuit's wires with a casing (1px bone-50 underlay for separation against faded neighbors) + the role-color stroke thickened to the existing .wire.is-active (3.5px + drop-shadow glow), while every other wire drops to --vt-recede (0.2). This is exactly the .wire.is-active / .wire.dim pair that ALREADY exists in topology.css — it just needs the step (not only pin-selection) to drive it.

Wire MOTION already encodes electrical state — keep it, it's a genuine differentiator over CAD. topology.css animates dash-flow speed by wire state (.wire.steady-12v flow 2.4s, .wire.signal-pegged 0.5s, .wire.pwm-max 0.22s) — animated current is something a static CAD diagram or a Snap-on PDF can't do, and it reads as 'live instrument.' Preserve it, but it MUST respect the existing @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) guard (already present) and the faded/receded wires must drop to animation:none (already true for .wire.off). The premium move: the ACTIVE circuit's wire animates at its true state speed; receded wires freeze. Motion becomes a focus cue, not ambient noise.
