realProductsCited.what: Linear deliberately made navigation 'a few notches dimmer' so the main work area takes precedence — 'not every element should carry equal visual weight; ones that support orientation should recede.' Uses LCH theming, Inter at a custom 510 weight, restraint over loudness.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: The articulated principle behind our spine: recede = lower visual weight for orientation elements, full weight for the work. Our context overlays (scenario state, captured/missing) are orientation chrome -> they live at fg-3/dimmed weight; the active circuit + the tapped part's reading carry full weight. Validates choosing OKLCH (we already use it) for evenly-stepped elevation/recede ramps.

realProductsCited.name: Mapbox route-line / transit styling
realProductsCited.what: A route line is three stacked layers — casing (wide dark underlay) -> main route line -> traffic overlay — and inactive legs are styled independently via styleInactiveRouteLegsIndependently + inActiveRouteLegsColor.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: The casing layer: render active-circuit wires with a 1px bone-50 underlay so the role-colored stroke separates cleanly from faded neighbor wires (otherwise color-on-color muddies). And the active-vs-inactive-leg split is exactly active-circuit-vs-receded-harness — a shipped, proven model for 'this segment of the path matters now, the rest is context.'

realProductsCited.name: ISO 2575 / SAE J578 automotive telltale color hierarchy
realProductsCited.what: International standard: amber/yellow = caution/advisory (check soon, approaching a limit, not yet critical); red = immediate danger / stop now. The meaning is consistent across every vehicle dashboard since 1978.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: Grounds our provenance + reading color semantics in the tech's existing mental model: amber 'needs field check' = 'we don't know yet, verify' (advisory) reads correctly to anyone who's seen a dash; red is RESERVED for an actual measured-wrong/fault reading (the P0087 low-pressure value). Never spend red on a data gap or it dilutes the one color that should mean 'this measurement is bad.'

concreteDirection: Adopt the existing 'Workshop Instrument' tokens wholesale (app/globals.css + components/vt/v2.css + v2-instruments.css) as the canvas's visual system — the canvas should be indistinguishable in palette/type/density from the curator desktop kit. Then make these specific additions/changes. (1) DEFINE the six missing wire-role tokens in globals.css :root as semantic tokens on the element ramp: --role-12v -> warm red near --vt-risk-high (power=hot), --role-ground -> --vt-fg-2 graphite (return=neutral), --role-signal -> --vt-elem-voltage, --role-5v-ref -> --vt-elem-coolant, --role-low-ref -> desaturated coolant, --role-pwm -> --vt-elem-vacuum. (2) Add --vt-recede:0.2 as the canonical 'faded background' opacity (nodes, wires, labels all fade to it on recede), reusing the value already baked into .wire.off/.wire.dim. (3) Replace the heavy-bordered .topo-node card with an engraved faceplate: 0.5px hairline (matching the kit's universal 0.5px rules), serif name, mono micro-cap kind/location, pins as the only high-contrast geometry; selection = navy ink halo + --vt-accent-bg tint, not a 2px amber border. (4) Type discipline: mono+tnum for every reading/number (the live 'now showing' value, expected range, pin numbers), Instrument Serif for the part name and the one-line Operational Theori, Inter Tight micro-caps for eyebrows only. (5) Provenance as a quiet semantic ramp: amber hairline+micro-label = 'needs field check', neutral graphite = 'inferred from theory', single navy tick = 'field-verified'; red is reserved for fault readings only. (6) Focus mechanic = Mapbox casing + KiCad/Altium net-highlight: active circuit wires get a bone underlay + the existing .wire.is-active 3.5px glow and animate at true state speed; the rest of the harness drops to --vt-recede and freezes animation. All step transitions use --vt-ease-instrument over --vt-dur-3/4 on opacity + viewport fit only — no bounce, no scale-pop.
critiqueOfCurrent: The current state is two visual systems colliding. The vt kit (globals.css/v2.css) is a genuinely premium, coherent 'blueprint-ink-on-bone' instrument system — but the topology canvas barely uses it. The react-flow canvas ships the rejected default-AI look head-on: a dotted <Background /> grid (line 113 of topology-diagram.tsx, exactly the 'decorative dotted grid' the brief bans), default <Controls />, colorMode='light' default chrome, and node boxes that are hard-bordered generic cards (.topo-node, 1.4px solid border, stacked text). Worse, the wire-color language — the single most important visual contract on a wiring diagram — is broken: every --role-* token is referenced but undefined, so wires render colorless and the 'color = electrical role' system silently does nothing. Selection is signaled with a flat 2px amber border that fights the amber=caution provenance meaning (amber is overloaded). The canvas sits inside a .topo grid as 'one box among equals' (grid-template-columns: 1fr 380px with header/scenario/readout/footer siblings) — the diagram is boxed with border+radius like a widget, not given the room. And nothing is step-aware: the only focus state (.wire.is-active/.dim) is driven by manual pin-selection, never by the wizard step, so the recede language that's HALF-BUILT in the CSS never fires for the actual diagnostic walk.
pitfalls: Do NOT mint a new palette. A second token set parallel to --vt-* is the fastest way to make the canvas feel like a bolted-on third-party widget instead of native Vyntechs. Every color the canvas needs already exists or maps cleanly onto the element/risk ramps.

Do NOT overload amber. Amber currently signals BOTH node selection (.topo-pin.is-selected, .topo-node.is-selected) AND the gap/provenance state. If amber means 'caution/unverified' (the domain-correct meaning per ISO 2575) it cannot also mean 'this is selected.' Use navy/signal for selection-focus, reserve amber strictly for 'needs field check.'

Do NOT use red for data gaps. Red (--vt-risk-high/destructive) must mean an actual measured-wrong/fault reading. If a missing data point is red, the tech can't distinguish 'we don't have this yet' from 'this circuit is failing' — and the P0087 fault reading loses its alarm color.

Do NOT keep the dotted react-flow <Background /> or default <Controls />. Both are the literal rejected default-AI look. If a ground is needed, the kit already has a near-invisible graph-paper grid (.vt-app background-image, oklch 0.022 alpha) that reads as bond paper, not an AI canvas.

Do NOT animate node entrances/exits with scale or bounce on step change. The motion contract is 'calm, no bounces.' Recede/focus is opacity + viewport fit only; bouncy reflows will read as toy-like and break the instrument feel.

Do NOT let wire-state dash animation run on receded/background wires — ambient motion across a faded harness defeats the focus and ignores prefers-reduced-motion intent. Active circuit animates; everything receded freezes (the CSS already freezes .wire.off — extend that to all receded wires).

Do NOT set readings in the serif body face or proportional figures. Numeric readings that re-render (live 'now showing' values) MUST be mono + tabular-nums or the digits jitter on update — the tell of a web page, not a gauge.
mobileNotes: The vt kit is explicitly desktop-only (v2.css header), so mobile theming is a genuine ADD, not a harvest — but the FOUNDATION tokens (color, type families, radii, motion) are viewport-agnostic and carry straight to 375-414px; only the desktop CHROME primitives (vt-topbar 52px, vt-twopane, vt-threepane, 240px sidebars) don't. For the canvas at phone width: (1) Wire-role color and --vt-recede behave identically — color is the cheapest focus cue and survives the small screen best, so lean on it harder when there's no room for pin labels. (2) The engraved-faceplate node must shed its mono micro-caps (kind/location) at phone width and keep only the serif name + role-colored pins — the existing topology.css already shrinks pin hit-targets but enforces a 32x28px minimum (line 341, 'mobile hit-target floor'); keep that floor, it's correct. (3) Type scale: the live reading stays mono+tnum but can step from the desktop size down one rung on the --vt-fs ramp (e.g. fs-14 not fs-16); never below 12px for a measured value the tech reads at arm's length under a hood. (4) Recede opacity should go SLIGHTLY deeper on mobile (~0.12-0.15 vs 0.2) because faded-but-present competes harder for attention on a small screen — but never to 0 (the spine says fade, not vanish). (5) Tabular numerals matter MORE on mobile, not less: a jittering reading on a 390px screen is more disruptive. (6) Provenance: the amber 'needs field check' hairline+micro-label survives fine; the quiet see-source affordance becomes a tap target (44px) rather than a hover, since there's no hover on touch.

lens: Mobile (375-414px) — the hard gate: full-bleed + step-aware on a phone a tech holds at the truck, one-handed, gloved, in glare
keyInsights: The phone is NOT a shrunk desktop — it's a different posture. A tech at the vehicle is one-handed (other hand holds a meter/probe), often gloved, in engine-bay glare, glancing not reading. The mobile design target is 'glanceable + one-thumb', so EVERY interactive thing the tech needs mid-test (the active part, the 'right now' reading, the scenario toggle) must sit in the bottom third (thumb green-zone), and every tap target must be >=48x48px with >=8px spacing (Material foundations / UXPin) — the current 24px pin chips with role+number stacked are un-tappable with gloves and must grow to fat pill targets on touch.

The map-navigation model is the right mental model for mobile, and it ALREADY solves our spine. Google's Navigation SDK reframes the camera on each step (followMyLocation auto-pans/zooms; showRouteOverview fits the relevant span) — the phone NEVER shows the whole route at reading size during a turn. Translate directly: on mobile, entering the lift-pump step should auto-`fitBounds` (react-flow `fitView` with a node-id filter) to ONLY the lift-pump circuit nodes + their edges, animated ~400ms. The tech does not pan-hunt for the circuit; the canvas drives itself to it. Free pan/zoom stays available (pinch/drag) but is the exception, not the entry state. (Google Navigation SDK camera modes.)

Tap-to-pull detail = a Maps-style bottom sheet with detents, NOT a full takeover and NOT the desktop right-aside reflowed. Use the iOS/Apple-Maps pattern: a PEEK detent (~120-160px, one-thumb, shows only the step-led fact) and an EXPAND detent (~half to ~75% screen) for the secondary connector/wires/Operational Theori. CRITICAL: set the equivalent of `largestUndimmedDetentIdentifier = .medium` — at peek/half the sheet is NON-modal with NO scrim, so the faded harness + the lift-pump circuit stay visible and the tech keeps their place in the whole truck. Only the expanded detent dims. (Apple UISheetPresentationController; Material persistent vs modal sheets.)

The bottom sheet must NEVER cover the part being worked on — this is the spine's rule #5 made literal on the most constrained screen. When a sheet opens to peek/half, shift the canvas camera UP by the sheet height so the active node (lift pump) re-centers in the VISIBLE region above the sheet. This is exactly Turo Engineering's 'adjust the Compose Google Map camera as the bottom sheet moves so the previous center stays centered' technique. Without it, the tech taps the lift pump and the sheet hides the lift pump — self-defeating. (Turo Engineering, Adjusting Compose Google Map While Bottom Sheet Moves.)

Fade/focus EARNS MORE on mobile than desktop, because less glass = recede is the only way to fit. On a 390px screen the full P0087 fuel harness (lift pump, FCA, CP4, injectors x8, PCM, rail-pressure sensor, fuse/relay) is illegible if all shown at reading size. So mobile should push the spine harder: faded parts drop to ~12-18% opacity AND collapse to dots/stubs (no labels) so they read as 'the rest of the truck is still there' orientation texture, while the lift-pump circuit renders at full size with labels. Desktop can afford faded-but-labeled; mobile cannot. The recede is a feature of the small screen, not a compromise.

Provenance has to survive glare and shrink without becoming a badge wall. Keep it as a single quiet inline line in the PEEK detent using the existing label map (TRAINING-INFERRED -> 'inferred from theory'; GAP -> amber 'needs field verification'; FIELD-VERIFIED -> 'field-verified') with a small chevron/'why?' that expands the see-source path only on demand (pull, not push). Amber 'needs field check' is the one provenance state that must read at a glance in sunlight — give it a high-contrast amber dot + text, not a pale pastel pill, because a gap is the one thing a tech must not miss before trusting a reading.

Scenario state is the one piece of persistent chrome mobile can't make full-bleed — solve it as a single collapsed control, not the desktop's 4-group ScenarioBar. At 390px the Ignition/Engine/Load/Fault rows wrap into a multi-line mess. Collapse to ONE compact status chip in the top bar reading the current state (e.g. 'Key on - engine off') that, on tap, opens a dedicated scenario bottom sheet with big thumb-zone segmented controls. The 'Now showing - <fault>' readout becomes a thin status strip pinned just above the detail sheet so the live state is always glanceable but never eats canvas. Reuse the existing data-driven compound-state derivation (deriveCompound/findOperationSlug) unchanged — only the presentation collapses.

Curator completeness ('X captured / Y missing') is curator-role-only AND should be a separate sheet/route on mobile, never the footer-overlay it is today. CapturedMissingFooter currently overlays the canvas bottom — on mobile that's exactly the thumb zone the live reading needs, and it leaks curator meta to no one's benefit during a real job. Gate it by role and move it behind a curator-only entry point so the technician's mobile canvas is 100% the diagram + step + reading. (Spine rule #6; current captured-missing-footer.tsx overlays inline on mobile per the screen comment.)

Mobile must keep the canvas as the BODY of the walk, not add a stepper. Per the standing 'no step N of M / no upcoming-work preview' principle, the only step affordance is ONE primary thumb-zone button on the sheet edge that advances the walk ('Reading logged - next' / 'Mark needs field check'). Tapping it triggers the next camera reframe (the canvas reframes to the next circuit, the way Maps advances to the next turn). The step IS the camera move + the led-fact; there is never a 'step 3 of 7' on screen.

realProductsCited.name: Google Maps / Navigation SDK (mobile turn-by-turn)
realProductsCited.what: Turn-by-turn nav reframes the camera per step: followMyLocation auto-pans/zooms/tilts to the active maneuver; showRouteOverview fits only the relevant span; the phone never shows the whole route at reading size during a turn. Camera perspective/zoom/bearing/tilt are all controllable per step.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: On mobile, entering a walk step auto-drives the canvas (react-flow fitView filtered to the step's node ids) to ONLY the lift-pump circuit, animated. Advancing the walk reframes to the next circuit. Free pinch/pan stays as the manual-override exception, not the default posture. This makes 'only what's needed now' literal: the canvas takes the tech to the parts instead of making them pan-hunt.

realProductsCited.name: Apple Maps / UISheetPresentationController detents
realProductsCited.what: Maps/Find My/Stocks use an always-present bottom sheet with medium (~50% height) and large detents; largestUndimmedDetentIdentifier = .medium keeps the map fully interactive (no scrim) until the sheet is expanded to full.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: Tap-a-part detail = a 2-detent bottom sheet. PEEK/HALF detents are non-modal with no scrim so the faded harness + active circuit stay visible (tech keeps their place); only the EXPANDED detent dims to read connector/wires/Operational Theori. Peek shows just the step-led fact (the lift-pump 12V-feed reading); expand reveals the secondary facts. Pull-not-push, on-tap, one-part — exactly the spine's tap contract.

realProductsCited.name: Turo Engineering — adjusting Google Map camera as the bottom sheet moves
realProductsCited.what: When a bottom sheet expands/collapses over a map, they shift the map camera so the previous center stays centered in the now-smaller visible region above the sheet, instead of letting the sheet cover the point of interest.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: When the detail sheet opens to peek/half, pan the canvas camera UP by the sheet height so the tapped node (lift pump) re-centers in the visible band ABOVE the sheet. This is the mechanism that satisfies the spine's 'context never sits on top of the part the tech is working on' on the most constrained screen.

realProductsCited.name: Material Design 3 — bottom sheets (persistent vs modal)
realProductsCited.what: Persistent sheets sit at the same elevation as content and let the background (map) stay interactive while panned; modal sheets add a scrim and block. Modal sheets cap initial height ~50% and expand to full with internal scroll; expandable sheets start small and grow.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: Use a persistent/expandable sheet (not a modal dialog) so the canvas stays live behind peek/half. Cap the initial detail at ~50% with internal scroll for the longer Operational Theori; the diagram is never fully occluded until the tech deliberately expands.

realProductsCited.name: NN/g — Bottom Sheets: Definition and UX Guidelines
realProductsCited.what: Always include a visible Close (X) button (don't rely only on swipe-down); support the back gesture; use sheets only for short interactions; do NOT stack sheets or replace page-to-page flows; sheets preserve substantial visibility of the underlying info (vs full-screen dialogs that force reorientation).
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: Detail sheet gets an explicit Close affordance plus swipe (the existing topo-panel__close X carries over). Never stack the scenario sheet on top of the detail sheet — opening one closes the other. The 'preserves visibility of underlying info' property is precisely why a sheet beats the full-takeover for a tech who must keep eyes on the circuit.

realProductsCited.name: Material Design foundations / UXPin — touch targets & thumb zone
realProductsCited.what: >=48x48dp (~9mm) targets with >=8px spacing; a 24px icon can keep its look but pad the hit area to 48px; primary actions belong in the bottom-third thumb green-zone for one-handed reach.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: Pin chips (currently 24px-ish role+number) get padded hit areas to >=48px on touch; the primary 'next/log' action and scenario toggle live in the bottom-third thumb zone. Gloves + one-handed = no top-corner-only controls.

realProductsCited.name: Shapr3D — mobile CAD canvas gestures (double-tap-to-fit-on-selection)
realProductsCited.what: Pinch=zoom, drag=pan, double-tap a face snaps the camera to frame that face; one hand can steer the view while the other edits — a proven phone/tablet canvas grammar for pro work.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: Adopt the same gesture grammar so it's instantly familiar: pinch-zoom / drag-pan for manual override, and double-tap a part to re-fit the camera to that part's circuit (a manual echo of the automatic step reframe). Keeps the canvas a real tool, not a static image.

realProductsCited.name: ColorUXLab — mobile color/contrast for sunlight ('Sunlight Boost')
realProductsCited.what: Outdoor glare lowers perceived contrast; high-contrast mobile palettes are practical not aggressive; a 'sunlight boost' compensates without enlarging type or increasing density.
realProductsCited.whatToSteal: Mobile palette must hold up in an engine bay: dark-surface canvas with high-contrast circuit lines, amber 'needs field check' as a saturated high-contrast token (not pastel), and the live reading in a bold high-contrast readout. Don't ship the current light-mode default-react-flow contrast outdoors.

concreteDirection: Mobile is a dark, full-bleed canvas with a self-driving camera and a 2-detent bottom sheet — modeled on Maps turn-by-turn, not on the desktop layout shrunk. Concrete layout for the P0087 lift-pump-12V-feed step on a 390px phone, top to bottom: (1) A thin top status bar (~44px): back chevron, vehicle short ('6.7L Power Stroke - P0087'), and a single tappable scenario chip reading the active state ('Key on - engine off'). No 4-group ScenarioBar. (2) The canvas fills everything between top bar and sheet. On entering this step it auto-reframes (fitView filtered to lift-pump circuit node ids: lift pump, its connector/pins, the 12V feed wire, ground, the feeding fuse/relay, plus the rail-pressure sensor as the downstream symptom marker), animated ~400ms. The rest of the fuel harness (FCA, CP4, 8 injectors, PCM) renders FADED to ~12-18% opacity AND collapsed to unlabeled dots/stubs — present as orientation texture, never competing. The active 12V-feed wire is the one highlighted line; its pin is the one fat (>=48px) tappable target. (3) A thin 'Now showing' status strip pinned just above the sheet (only when a scenario/fault is active). (4) A persistent bottom sheet at PEEK detent (~140px, non-modal, no scrim): line 1 = the STEP-LED fact for this moment — the lift-pump 12V-feed expected reading ('Key-on: ~12V at the feed pin') with its quiet provenance line ('inferred from theory' / amber 'needs field check'); line 2 = the one primary thumb-zone action ('Reading logged - next' which triggers the next camera reframe, plus a quieter 'mark needs field check'). When the tech taps the lift-pump pin, the sheet rises to HALF (~50%, still non-modal) AND the canvas camera pans up so the lift pump re-centers ABOVE the sheet (Turo technique); the HALF sheet now shows secondary facts in step-order: connector, # wires/pins + what each does, location, then the short Operational Theori, with a 'why do we believe this?' chevron for the see-source path. Expanding to FULL (only on deliberate drag) dims the canvas for deep reading. Gestures: pinch-zoom, drag-pan, double-tap-a-part to re-fit (Shapr3D grammar). Palette: dark surface, high-contrast circuit lines, saturated amber for gaps, bold readout — built to survive engine-bay glare.
critiqueOfCurrent: The current screen is a vertical stack of co-equal blocks (header, ScenarioBar, 'Now showing' strip, boxed react-flow, overlaid captured/missing footer, right aside that becomes a bottom sheet) — on mobile this is the worst case: a tech scrolls past four chrome blocks to reach a cramped, light-mode, dotted-grid canvas showing the ENTIRE harness at illegible size with zero step-awareness (selection is a flat empty|component|pin|connection explorer, topology-diagnostic.tsx). The 4-group ScenarioBar (Ignition/Engine/Load/Fault) wraps into a wrapping mess at 390px. The CapturedMissingFooter overlays the canvas bottom — eating the exact thumb-zone band the live reading needs, and leaking curator-only completeness meta to a technician who shouldn't see it (violates spine rule #6). The detail aside-as-bottom-sheet is the one right instinct, but today it's a flat field dump (Location/Function/Electrical/probe points/tests/pins all at once) with no step-led ordering, no peek detent, and — worst — nothing shifts the canvas so the sheet covers the very part just tapped. 24px pin chips are un-tappable with gloves. Nothing reframes between steps because there are no steps. It reads, on a phone, as a web article with a diagram embedded in it — the literal opposite of the spine.
pitfalls: Sheet-covers-the-part: opening the detail sheet over the tapped node so the tech taps the lift pump and the sheet hides it. Must pan the camera up by sheet height (Turo technique) on every detent change.

Modal scrim at peek/half: dimming the canvas the moment the sheet opens kills the spine's 'keep your place in the whole truck'. Peek/half MUST be non-modal/no-scrim; only the deliberate FULL expand dims.
Shrinking the desktop ScenarioBar instead of collapsing it: the 4 control groups wrap into an unusable multi-line block at 375-390px. Collapse to one status chip + a dedicated scenario sheet.

Showing the whole harness at reading size 'so they don't lose context': illegible on 390px and re-introduces the exact mid-job clutter the spine bans. Recede must be HARDER on mobile (collapse faded parts to dots), not softer.
Tap targets under 48px (today's 24px pin chips): unusable gloved or in motion; causes mis-taps that break trust mid-test.
Leaking curator completeness to techs: keep CapturedMissingFooter role-gated and off the technician's canvas entirely; never as a bottom overlay in the thumb zone.
Stacking sheets (NN/g): opening the scenario sheet on top of the detail sheet. Opening one must close the other.

Light-mode default react-flow (dotted grid + default node boxes) outdoors: low perceived contrast in glare, plus it's the rejected default-AI aesthetic. Ship a dark high-contrast canvas with the dotted background removed.

A visible stepper / progress ('step 3 of 7') sneaking onto the small screen because 'there's a wizard underneath': violates the standing no-step-N-of-M principle. The camera reframe + one led-fact IS the step.
Auto-reframe with no manual escape: if the tech can't pinch/pan to verify a neighboring part, the self-driving camera becomes a cage. Keep free gesture override always available (Shapr3D grammar).
mobileNotes: This entire lens IS the mobile note. Net adaptation from desktop: (a) the desktop right-aside detail becomes a 2-detent persistent bottom sheet (peek ~140px / half ~50% / full on drag), non-modal until full, with the canvas camera shifting up so the tapped part stays visible above it; (b) the desktop 4-group ScenarioBar collapses to one top-bar status chip that opens a dedicated scenario sheet with big thumb-zone segmented controls (reusing deriveCompound/findOperationSlug logic untouched); (c) step transitions = automatic react-flow fitView reframes to the step's circuit node ids (Maps camera model), with faded parts dropped to ~12-18% opacity AND collapsed to unlabeled dots (recede harder than desktop because there's no room); (d) all touch targets padded to >=48px, primary 'next/log' action + scenario chip in the bottom-third thumb zone for one-handed gloved use; (e) dark, high-contrast, glare-resistant palette with saturated amber for 'needs field check'; (f) curator completeness role-gated off the technician canvas entirely; (g) provenance is one quiet inline line with an on-demand 'why?' see-source expansion (pull, not push); (h) gesture grammar = pinch-zoom / drag-pan / double-tap-to-fit (Shapr3D) so manual override of the self-driving camera is always one gesture away.

lens: Felt value per interaction — how each tap/read/transition feels satisfying, fast, and trustworthy for a master diesel tech mid-job, with provenance honesty as a FELT quality
keyInsights: TAP MUST ANSWER WITHIN ONE FRAME, NOT WAIT FOR A PANEL. Every piece of data the canvas can show is already loaded synchronously by loadSystemTopology (components, pins, scenarios, pinReadings, missingLogic are all in-memory maps in topology-diagnostic.tsx — componentById/pinById/connectionById are useMemo'd Maps). There is no network round-trip on tap. So the felt-value bar is not 'fast enough' — it is INSTANT. Linear's whole reputation is built on sub-100ms optimistic response where 'animation starts as soon as you click, in parallel to the actual work' (simonhearne optimistic-ui). The recede/focus motion should BEGIN on pointerdown, not on the click event resolving — the tech should feel the canvas lean toward the part before their finger lifts.

THE TAP IS A DECLARATION OF INTENT, SO THE FEEDBACK MUST BE A COMMITMENT, NOT A HOVER. The brief is explicit: a tap = the tech saying 'tell me about THIS part.' The felt difference between a satisfying pro tool and a web widget is that the satisfying one commits visibly: the tapped part should get a single decisive state change (a crisp focus ring + the rest of the harness settling back), not a soft web-y hover glow. Borrow Things 3's 'glassy buttons that respond with a subtle glow and scale' and its Magic Plus 'liquid deform' (culturedcode) — ONE confident gesture per tap. The current topo-node.tsx only toggles an `is-selected` class with a box-shadow transition (topology.css:94); that reads as a checkbox, not a commitment. Make the focus event feel like the canvas turning to face the part.

PROVENANCE HONESTY BECOMES TRUSTWORTHY WHEN IT IS A TEXTURE, NOT A BADGE. The hard constraint says 'inferred from theory' / 'needs field check' must read as evidence-carrying, never as hedging or a loud badge wall. Today GAP renders as an amber pill on the node (topo-node__gap 'needs field check', topology.css:358-362) and a bordered chip in the panel — that is exactly the verdict-pill anti-pattern the memory rejects. The felt-trust move: encode source grade in the PART'S OWN RENDERING, the way a tech reads a real wiring diagram — field-verified parts draw at full ink weight, 'inferred from theory' parts draw at slightly lighter stroke/desaturated, GAP parts draw with a hairline dashed edge (the universal 'provisional/not-to-scale' convention in real schematics and CAD). The grade is felt as confidence-in-the-drawing before a single word is read. This is calibrated honesty made visual — the tool literally draws less boldly where it knows less.

RECEDE = FADE MUST BE A SETTLING, NOT A SWITCH — DAMPED LIKE A NEEDLE. When the step changes or a part is tapped, the irrelevant harness should EASE to its faint background state with a damped curve, never a hard opacity flip. Analog instrument design is explicit that a trustworthy gauge needle 'should behave like a speedometer — smooth with no jerks... it makes no sense if the needle flutters' (NI/ISS Auto). A hard cut feels like a glitch; a damped settle (the existing --vt-ease token applied at ~240-320ms with a slight overshoot-free ease-out) feels like the instrument responding to a real input. The wires already animate flow keyed to electrical state (topology.css:291-302, dash-flow timing maps voltage/PWM to speed) — that living-current quality is the single best felt asset already in the build; the recede motion must feel like it belongs to the same physical world: faded wires slow their flow and dim to 0.16 (the existing `.wire.off` opacity), focused wires keep flowing at full strength.

THE 'RIGHT NOW' READING IS THE EMOTIONAL CORE — IT MUST FEEL LIKE A LIVE METER, NOT A TEXT FIELD. For the P0087 lift-pump-12V-feed step, the one fact the tech is chasing is the reading at that pin. The panel currently shows it as a labeled prose block (topo-panel__right-now, detail-panel.tsx:251-265). The felt upgrade: render the active reading in the mono tabular-figure face the kit already defines (font-feature-settings:'tnum', used in vt-stat__num / vt-plan-step__conf-num, v2-instruments.css) at instrument scale, with the expected range shown as a quiet band around it so a wrong value reads as WRONG instantly — the satisfaction of a meter where you don't have to parse, you just SEE 'should be ~12V, reading 0.3V.' iA Writer's focus mode 'dims everything except the sentence you are currently writing' (ia.net) — here, the active reading is that one sentence; everything else on the part's detail dims behind it.

SEE-SOURCE MUST BE A QUIET PULL, REWARDED ONLY WHEN ASKED — THE SAME PULL-NOT-PUSH RULE AS THE DETAIL ITSELF. Trust is built by the path EXISTING, not by the evidence shouting. Provenance research confirms the credible pattern is 'place source links near referenced content, clear labels, consistent format' and that synthesized data must 'explain the generation process' on demand (DataONE / arxiv ProvenanceLens). The felt design: the source grade word ('inferred from theory') is itself the affordance — tapping it expands the why-we-believe-this inline, in place, then collapses. No standing 'sources' column. This mirrors the part-detail being pulled not pushed, so the whole tool has ONE consistent felt grammar: nothing speaks until you ask it to, and when you ask, it answers in place and gets out of the way. Reinforces 'AI is a tool with evidence, never an oracle' without ever saying the word.

PERCEIVED PERFORMANCE = THE CANVAS NEVER GOES BLANK BETWEEN STEPS. Google Maps navigation never cuts to black between maneuvers — it 'animates the camera with quadratic ease-out' to reframe on the next turn while the road stays continuous (developers.google.com maps camera). The step-aware canvas should do the same: when the walk advances, the SAME diagram reframes (pan/zoom-to-relevant + recede) rather than re-rendering a new scene. The tech keeps their place in the truck (the spine's 'keep their place in the whole truck' rule) AND the transition reads as the tool moving with them, not reloading. Continuity is the deepest felt-trust signal: a tool that never blinks is a tool that knows where it is.

MOTION RESTRAINT IS ITSELF A TRUST SIGNAL FOR A PRO. Linear's refresh principle is 'don't compete for attention you haven't earned' and 'if most people don't immediately notice what changed, that's a good sign' (linear.app/now). A master tech distrusts a tool that performs. The felt target is the OP-1 / Teenage Engineering bar — 'limit tweakable parameters to the essentials so you're never diverted from what matters' (teenage.engineering) — every animation must do diagnostic work (showing current flow, showing focus, showing a wrong reading), never decoration. No spring bounces, no confetti, no easing that calls attention to itself. The satisfaction comes from precision and inevitability, the way a good torque wrench clicks — not from flourish.

RESPECT prefers-reduced-motion AS A FIRST-CLASS FEEL, NOT AN ACCESSIBILITY AFTERTHOUGHT. The wire-flow animations already gate on it (topology.css:317-318 sets animation:none under reduced-motion). Extend the same discipline: under reduced-motion the recede/focus becomes an instant-but-still-damped-in-feel opacity/weight change with NO pan-zoom camera move (camera moves are the most nausea-prone). A shop tech on a phone in a bright bay with motion sensitivity should get the same INSTANT, CALM clarity — the felt value (focus + trust) survives even when the motion is stripped. This is the proof the felt-value is in the information design, not the animation.

concreteDirection: Build the felt experience on three timed, damped beats, all powered by data that is already loaded in memory (zero tap-time network) so the bar is INSTANT, not merely fast. (1) FOCUS-ON-TAP: on pointerdown, the canvas commits — the tapped part gets a single decisive focus state (subtle glow + ~1.02 scale, Things-3-confident not web-hover-soft, replacing the flat is-selected box-shadow), the rest of the harness DAMPED-settles back to faint (reusing the existing .wire.off 0.16 opacity + slowed dash-flow), and the part's detail surface appears anchored to the pin (a hairline leader line from the actual pin to the detail, Figma-measurement style) rather than in a detached right aside. Step-aware ordering: lead the detail with the one fact the step needs (the lift-pump 12V-feed reading), rendered at instrument scale in the kit's tnum mono face with the expected range as a quiet band so a wrong value reads as WRONG at a glance; connector / #pins / what-each-wire-does / Operational Theori sit dimmed below (iA Focus-Mode hierarchy), pulled into full weight only on further tap. (2) RECEDE-AS-SETTLE: every step/selection change eases over ~240-320ms ease-out (the existing --vt-ease/--vt-dur tokens), the SAME diagram reframing/dimming like Google Maps zooming to the next maneuver — never a blank re-render, never a hard flip; the living current-flow wires stay the physical anchor (focused circuit flows full-strength, receded wires slow and dim). (3) PROVENANCE-AS-INK: encode source grade in how the PART is drawn, not in a pill — field-verified = full ink weight, inferred-from-theory = lighter/desaturated stroke, gap = hairline dashed edge (real-schematic 'provisional' convention); the grade WORD ('inferred from theory') is the quiet see-source affordance, tapping it expands why-we-believe-this inline then collapses (pull-not-push, same grammar as the part detail). Honor prefers-reduced-motion as a first-class path: instant-but-calm weight/opacity changes, no camera pan-zoom, same focus + same trust. Every animation must pass the OP-1 test — it does diagnostic work (shows current, shows focus, shows a wrong reading) or it is cut.
critiqueOfCurrent: The current stacked-web-page state fails the felt-value bar at every interaction. (1) The tap feedback is a flat box-shadow toggle on the node (topo-node.tsx is-selected, topology.css:94) — it reads like ticking a checkbox in a form, not a tool committing to the part the tech declared intent on; there is zero sense of the canvas RESPONDING to me. (2) Detail is pushed into a detached right-hand <aside> (topo-panel, detail-panel.tsx:294) that is spatially disconnected from the pin on the canvas — the eye has to leave the part, cross the screen, and re-anchor, killing the felt continuity; on mobile it is a bottom sheet that covers the very part being worked. (3) The 'right now' reading is rendered as a labeled prose block (detail-panel.tsx:251-265) instead of an instrument value — the tech has to READ it instead of SEEING wrong-vs-right, so the emotional core of the diagnosis (is this pin good or bad?) carries no felt weight. (4) Provenance is the rejected verdict-pill anti-pattern: GAP is an amber chip on the node ('needs field check', topo-node__gap, topology.css:358) and a bordered pill in the panel — it SHOUTS hedging instead of being felt as honest confidence-in-the-drawing; it reads as the tool apologizing, not as evidence. (5) There is no motion vocabulary tying it together — selection just toggles classes; nothing settles, nothing reframes, the canvas never feels like it moves WITH the tech between steps. The whole thing has the feel of a web page with a widget in it (the brief's own words) precisely because no single interaction is satisfying, fast-feeling, or trust-building on its own.
pitfalls: Don't let provenance honesty turn into a badge wall — the moment 'inferred from theory' / 'needs field check' become persistent colored pills (as today's topo-node__gap does), they read as hedging/apology and INVERT trust. Felt-trust comes from the part being drawn with less ink where the tool knows less, not from a chip announcing doubt.

Don't add motion that performs. A master tech distrusts a tool that bounces, springs, or confettis. Any easing that calls attention to itself (overshoot, elastic, long durations >350ms) reads as a toy. Fails both the Linear 'don't notice the change' bar and the OP-1 essentials-only bar.
Don't make the focus/recede a hard opacity flip — a jerky cut reads as a glitch and erodes trust exactly like a fluttering gauge needle. Always damp the settle.

Don't push the detail surface as a standing panel — that re-violates the resolved pull-not-push rule and the theory-of-operation-is-source principle. It must appear on-tap, anchored to the part, and get out of the way; never a persistent right rail that competes with the canvas.

Don't reframe the camera (pan/zoom) under prefers-reduced-motion — camera moves are the most nausea-prone; degrade to instant-but-damped weight/opacity changes so the felt clarity survives without the motion.

Don't show the live reading as undifferentiated prose — if the tech must read and parse 'reading 0.3V' against 'expected ~12V' as two text rows, the wrong-vs-right judgment carries no felt weight. Render it as a value against a band so WRONG is seen, not computed.

Don't trigger focus motion on click-resolve when you could trigger on pointerdown — waiting for the click event to settle adds perceptible lag on a tool that has all data in memory; the canvas should lean toward the part before the finger lifts.

Don't let the 'see source / why we believe this' expansion become a wall of architecture-facts theory rows — it stays short, in-place, every-word-counts, and collapses; a dump re-crosses into the rejected push pattern.
